speaker: interviewee
text: If you want a cookie, go in and get it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready? OK. Gisela Konopka. Tape number seven. The banners were out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the banners were out, but the-- I forgot what the word is-- the head, who is supposed to marry you or whatever it is-- refused, because we were one-- one Jew and one non-Jew. And he knew the Germans would come and he didn't want to get into trouble with that. So he refused.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Croix-de-Feu. Does that mean something to some people? C-R-O-I-X. And then a new word. D-E. And then F-E-U. That is the French fascist. So he was a French fascist, and he wouldn't allow us to get married. So we still weren't getting married. Then, to make it very quick, somebody sent us into another village where there was a woman who wanted a couple to work. We thought that would be just fine. I said, finally, I might even have some children. Isn't that wonderful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when she-- and there we saw something, I think I should say that. It was in a village when everybody else hardly had any food, that woman had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the village was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I forgot. But there was no refugee there either. And she didn't want people like that. That was much too dangerous. So that was the end of that. So, back to Lavit, which was still decent to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now we are in '40 and '41. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, I will try to make this as fast as I can. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first visa under the special-- and I thought I wrote that down, but I can't remember that-- it's the president's-- the president had a commission for people who were especially under danger. Usually quite famous people with famous names. We were not famous. We had no famous names, but people vouched for us, what we had done and so on. But it is the President's Commission.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they sent some visas. But the first one who got the visa was I, not Paul. I refused. I didn't want to go. Finally we are together, you know, what's the use? And Paul, as usual, no, we have to tell the world. The Nazis will want to invade the whole world. Somebody has to be there to tell. You go. I don't know whether I will live, but you go. And I will do what I can do here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got, meanwhile, to know some very wonderful people in the village who listened to the clandestine time radio. So, and Paul worked as a wood cutter for very decent guy there. And they said, don't worry, we'll hide him. And they showed us some places that they had hiding places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of this is still in Lavit. Near Lavit. But they said, when you go we will hide him. Don't worry. We'll take care of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I had to leave. And when you had to leave to get your visa for the United States, you had to go to Marseilles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Marseilles there were committees that helped refugees. One of them was the Unitarian Committee and that's Fry-- F-R-Y-- who has saved lots of people. Erich Lewinsky worked with them as a refugee. I got on the train finally in Mont-Tremblant. Did you ever meet Fry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's coming. I got on the train in Mont-Tremblant. And-- but a snowstorm that will-- nobody believe that hits us at all. You know, that's known for from-- golf and is all the sun and all that. A snow storm, the train cannot go on, we have no food. There are soldiers there with their legs were blue, frozen, and I had to rub them. And we sat there for a day or two. And I thought, I'll never get the visa because I passed the deadline to go there. I'm saying that for a special reason. So we are-- I'm coming much too late because of that snowstorm and all in Marseilles. And the first thing they say when you go to the Council, come on. We have a good coat for you and we have a hat. That coat was worn by every woman who had to go before the Council. It was either made longer or shorter. The hat was worn by every woman, because once the Council said he wouldn't give visa to beggars. We came looking awful, you can be sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it's a little ridiculous, but I have to say that. But Fry and Lewinsky said, you go there. So I go there, and they say, well, you get the visa, but you have to go back and bring three photos. I had none. So I go back and they say, that's impossible, because they give you only a visitor visa. And for visitor visa, you don't need those three photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they telephone and they said yesterday the news came from the President's Committee to give immigration visas. So if I hadn't been in that snow storm, it would have been much harder for me. These are what I call my little miracles. So, very strange.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- so I would have to go, and there were some other refugees, with them through Spain and Portugal to get a boat in Lisbon. And that committee got the money together, because none of us had any money anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fry, I had no idea who he was. Was only a name. But I remembered that everybody talked with enormous adoration about him, you know? And I suddenly was called into his office. And never anybody seemed to be called into his office. So what does he want?

speaker: interviewee
text: And people say, how did he look, and you know, this is the problem. You forget. I remember somebody very tall. But maybe I'm wrong. I don't know anymore. Anyhow, so he says to me, I know you have done an awful lot of things, and you will-- maybe you don't want to do it. But we have a list that we got only yesterday of the people who are in greatest danger, and it has to be brought immediately-- I mean, not immediately. It takes two weeks to get to the United States. But it can't-- we cannot send it by pouch. We cannot send it by anything. It has to be brought into the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to carry it by pouch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. It can't be brought by diplomatic channels. So we have to send it through somebody, and would you be willing to take that? And I promise you, he said, that I know how to hide things. I tell you, he sure did. I think today one can probably say it. I don't know. Can one? It was thin paper, as usual. [LAUGHS] I'm knowing a lot about thin paper. But it was with a fine needle inserted into a toothpaste. What-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tube?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tube?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a tube of toothpaste. The tube looks totally untouched. And that's where I carried that. So I'm kind of proud of that. I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the letter was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's the names. It were just the names of all these people who were under enormous danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: To whom was it going?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you bring it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know anymore either. All I know is I was caught at the-- when we arrived at some refugee committee, and somebody said. And I knew it was legitimate, but I can't tell you that anymore. This is ridiculous. There's thing's that memory doesn't want to find.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Fry put the list into the toothpaste tube?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just received the tube of toothpaste.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it ready made. Ready made. He said it's in there, and we would appreciate it that you take it. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you remembered about Fry? Was he a nice person? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I liked him, but I can't say much. If I-- these were not times I think Fry-- today I would remember. But I do liked him. I liked-- he sounded so serious. I liked that. And he sounded concerned. And what I liked, that I remember, was that he was gentle about my having to do that. Because he felt it was, you know, dangerous, but you will do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he feel official? Like an official--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --representative of the United States government?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, was a good man. That was-- that I remember. But I can't tell much more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see his face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your first exposure to an American person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, probably. But I don't think I even thought about that. No, the Council was the first American. And he was not very nice. He has a fabulous name. I have that paper somewhere. But he wasn't very nice. But we were dirt. We looked like dirt. I think you mustn't forget that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Marseilles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now, with a few other people, and I don't know anymore exactly who they were. And, very quick. Spain was a catastrophe. Now, I knew that Spain is fascist. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't describe it. We had to wait for-- for another train, and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you walk through the streets, you would see children with blown-up bellies with hunger, and, behind them, the stores were full of food. And that was Spain. I saw that with my own eyes. And when we got into the place where they check your passport and all that stuff, there was a woman. And I carried a bag of bread from France. And she said, you don't need that anymore. And I said, I guess not. Please. So here's an official, you know, she's the one who checked you out, who wanted that bread. In addition to that-- I mean that's Spain to me, at that time. In addition to that, I remember that when we-- we wanted tickets. We needed tickets. No, we don't give you tickets to Portugal. But the man-- one of the men would traveled at the same time, he was apparently well-instructed. He pulled out some cigarettes and put them in. We got the tickets.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are train tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Train tickets. And when we sat in the train, behind-- I'll never forget that-- behind the train were children reaching out with their hands and begging for bread. Anything we had, we threw out. We said it will be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, strange. Very strange. Portugal immediately looked different from Spain. Very different. Not so many soldiers. Not so much police.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- in Portugal, I was placed-- and this is the ISK did these things. Was placed with a woman, a young woman about my age, I think. And we were to wait for the boat, which would always take a few days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not know one word of Portuguese. She didn't know any language that I spoke. And we communicated very nicely. And she bought me a piece of-- I'll never forget-- beige material and made me a dress. This is the dress I wore when I got married in the United . States. But it was just unbelievable, because I had nothing. And-- now I'm going fast. The boat, I still remember, was Nyassah. N-Y-A-S-S-A-H. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a trip to the United States. And I always said I would never in my life return to Europe, which isn't true. But, it took over two weeks because we had to cross minefields and go around them. I was always in the bottom of the boat because we were filled with people. This was filled with refugees. And I was vomiting all the time. I was sick all the time. And everybody had a story to tell that was pretty horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I remember was it was supposed to be a freighter, which a lot of room in the belly. And we were in there. That's all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: One large room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were all lined there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, if I would remember, but something. See, I never know how we slept, because we slept on stone, we slept on everything. I think it's just a mat or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume so, but I don't remember that either. It's odd. I don't remember hardly anything. I remember one young girl who was a teenager. I'm always interested in youngsters. And she was so excited about going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to tell, I didn't-- many of us were not. We didn't know anything about America. We didn't think-- we had-- I mean, I have to know, I knew America from Sinclair Lewis, and that wasn't the nicest, you know? So I didn't know. We just said, we'll go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that girl was so excited because she said, you know what-- I have never forgotten that-- you can paint your fingernails red. Real red. It was a little Jewish girl. She was wonderful. And then she said, and you know somebody told me I can work in a store and can get $5 a week. I mean, she was really life. [LAUGHS] And there was her [INAUDIBLE] mother sitting there, crying, because her father was killed, meanwhile. And this is the way I remember, but I don't remember very much. I cannot. This is odd.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two weeks. We arrived in New York harbor. The boat stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- we suddenly saw all people running up with photo-- with cameras. Suddenly, I and many others of us were hiding immediately. We thought we would never do that. Somebody will find out where we are. Somebody will tell somebody there. No. No. No. No. So we were hiding from them. Some nice, old men came, and later I learned this was the Jewish Labor Committee people. And they were so gentle. And they said, welcome. So that was nice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They came onto the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were allowed to come on. Then I do remember that we-- you know, I never went to Ellis Island. This was just straight off, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were all together. We were going off the boat, right? But before we were allowed getting a stamp that we were in, we had to show that we had the means. [LAUGHS] I think that's very funny, because it's $5. And that $5 went from hand to hand to hand to hand to hand. I don't know how many people got in on those one, $5 bill.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I'm sure there was nice men brought those in. And then it went, you know, each one who came had this $5 bill. And then it went to the next hand. That's the way we got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember very lively that somebody said, something to declare? And I said no. And they waved you through. And I turned to the next one, would you believe it? They believe you here? That was a miracle. Somebody trusts you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people from the refugee committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is-- now I know it. And I knew the date. Oh, dear. I have that somewhere. Well, I don't know. But it's 1941. It's spring, 1941. I know that. And there was a refugee committee. They were wonderful. They took us to a place that-- They had already found a place for us in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish refugee committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, I came to the Jewish refugee committee. When Paul-- that's later. We had to go to another one. But I was first at the Jewish refugee committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, now, people always say, how was it? First, it was very overwhelming. You don't know anything about New York-- oh, when we get into New York harbor, my first-- I almost yelled. I said, there are trees! Because we thought there would be only big buildings. So it was wonderful to see green and trees, which was very exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had arranged for all refugees-- it was one of those brownstone houses on the-- I forget, the 70s. I would have to look it up. In New York. And there was a couple that had that house and they rented rooms. And they were very friendly to us. And we got into a room. Each small room, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were by yourself at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. I was all by myself. Other people were also by themselves. I remember one-- I'm so angry that I can think of the name. It was a father and a daughter. The daughter later, I know, became a famous artist in Paris. But I can't think of her name. Because I saw an exhibit years later of her. But all kinds of people. Mostly labor people. I mean, this was the bunch with which I came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were very nice, and I'm sure we got something to eat. But I don't remember that. I do remember that there-- oh yeah, there were already refugees who had been there earlier. Now you get into more familiar territory. That means there are Eva Lewinsky. That's the sister of Erich Lewinsky. And there were all kinds of people who had been earlier refugees there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you carry the tube of toothpaste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I don't know. No use.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It got there. That I know. But who took it, I don't know. I really don't. There's no use that I try. I wrack my brain on that. If somebody would be still alive, but they are all dead. I'm living too long. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, to go very fast, I think the refugee committee was very good. People always complain about, but I never forgot. Really, I had nothing to wear. I mean, you come with one-- the skirt and the blouse, you washed in the river all the time. And that's what you come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had a place. You arrive and they say, you need clothing? Yes. All right. There was a place-- it was to me, I thought it was a department store. It was filled, you know, with clothes, beautifully hung up. Everything clean. Perfect. Choose three dresses. Choose six-- six pairs of stockings. What do I need six pairs of stockings for? Three underwear. I mean, it was just-- They told us that we should take that. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she typed that out on a piece of paper, and I said, I don't need all that. Oh yes, you need it. In America, people want to look nice. It was not real. I mean, that was very decent.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the other hand, the first social worker when I later taught social work, I didn't think was so hot. Because she said to me, how much money do you have in your pocket? Well, I'm a very honest person. And I said, $5 because I had those. She says, well then, you get along for a week. Now, you know, it was cheap at that time. But you had to also want to pay rent and all that. Where did that money come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the refugee committee somewhere gave us that when we arrived. After that $5 went through every hand, we got some money. Yes, there was support by the refugee committee. We were not allowed to go in public, you know that, at that time. No public assistance. Not at all. Which the refugee helped. But I would not want to, and lots of others didn't either. So I think it was only-- oh, when she said you can do that for a week, I said, no, no. This isn't for me. So I checked out, and across the hall was something that said, jobs. So, I checked and I had a job the second day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I was taking care of a baby in somebody's house. And I did something terrible. I've never forget and that poor baby. I couldn't understand why that baby couldn't get the nipple getting her orange juice. And then I realized that I hadn't strained the orange juice. So that's very bad, and I have to tell that about myself. But I finally thought about it. But that was only a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then for quite awhile I got a fine job. I got $8 a week for 12 hours of work every day, with the exception of Sunday. And it was in the-- in-- I think it's the Bronx. Where's that big bridge going over? I think it's Bronx. Oakland or the Bronx. I don't know anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and it was an old man, and I have to wash him, and to put clothes on him, and wash the teeth, which he could take out, and so on. I mean, taking care of that old man, but all day. There were all things to do. But then I realized that he had a son who had sleeping sickness. He was-- the saliva was always coming out. And he couldn't eat. I was really shocked. So I started feeding that one, too. And finally I washed him, too, because I just couldn't see that. But that was a regular job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you speaking English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But lousy. I spoke French fluently, and then there's this third language now, the English. And I had learned English, so I could read English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many-- in Germany. Many years earlier. But I had learned English so I could to read it, and I could-- but it was not very good. And I-- people always tell all the bad stories, but I think there's some nice stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never forget. I went the third or second day, I was in New York and I wanted to go somewhere. And I asked the man how to get there, you know, with my hands and with my English. And he, very slowly, walked with me, showed with me, and he says, verstehst-- which is German-- and says, do you understand? That was very funny. And he had fun doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm just giving one more example. I go into a store and I want some stamps. And I say, three stamps for five cents. And he says, you learn to say [GERMAN]. So the people weren't mean. I think they were friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences with anti-Semitism when you arrived in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me say-- no. Not in New York. But also I have to say, I came kind of, you know, not with a wave. I came with a wave of refugees. And they were both Jews and non-Jews. And we were treated very decently. No, not that. But, friends had told me that in the United States, they want you to kind of plan for something. And I was a teacher, originally. And so I said, go to the teachers union, or whatever it was, and talk with somebody. And I went there, and the woman right away said, no, no, no, no. We had enough of those that come from other countries. I don't want-- we don't-- we don't need more teachers. I mean that was it. Whether it was only Jews, I don't know. But it sure sounded like it. But that was-- that was about it. Otherwise, no. The people where we stayed, the name was Clark. And later I learned that he was a journalist for the which was wonderful. But I had no idea. His wife was a psychologist. I was very afraid of talking to people. I always ran past him into my room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day he caught me and said, why are you running always? And I said, well. What do you think of [INAUDIBLE]? Oh, boy, I thought now. I said, well. He said, come on. I think he's awful. You know? So, but, I mean, as I began to know them, but it took time. You didn't trust people very easily. But they were wonderful people. They became very close friends of ours. You didn't trust people readily because of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. You were afraid of people. You didn't know whether they were spies. Somebody would tell somebody to the Germans what this was. You know. America wasn't in the war. And I-- should I go on? Is this--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in touch with Paul, I would imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, this is the committee that gets in touch with Paul. I hardly can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which committee was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still from-- What is it? It's-- refugee committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: The President's?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Emergency-- Emergency Rescue Committee. And people from the ISK. To make that very quick, it's four weeks or more later that Paul finally-- he got his visa. He got on a boat. I'll quit tell about that. But we are in no way at the--
